summary: Exhaustively discover GLM-5.2 decode speedup hacks beyond the CPU∥GPU split (16 angles → verify → ranked roadmap)
logs: Shortlist: AVX-512 + VNNI Q2_K/Q3_K expert vec_dot kernels (Zen5 full-width) | Q3_K row-interleaved repack (q3_K_8x8) + AVX-512 gemv, CPU experts in repack buffer | Re-enable CUDA kernel fusion (MUL_MAT_ID+GLU, attn) in the 2-GPU config | Cross-layer router-predicted GPU-branch expert prefetch (double-buffered ring) | Dedicated copy-engine stream for GPU-branch expert H2D | Q8-input-once + true async pinned join/prestage copies (kill per-layer sync tax) | Coalesce gate+up+down into one contiguous H2D + drop dead indexer weight from VRAM | -ub 64 prefill-buffer shrink + correct the wrong non-MLA KV budget to free a resident layer

roadmap.headline: 43 t/s is reachable; 52 is a stretch that needs the dual-GPU copy path AND a real kernel win to both land. The decisive fact, verified against your own spec's latest clean warm measurement (CPU_GPU_SPLIT_SPEC.md:281-290), is that the brief's "20ms CPU / 19ms GPU" split is superseded: cmp_cpu (offloaded-expert Q2_K/Q3_K dequant+matmul) is 29.5ms = 74% of decode, while in_gpu (attention + all resident experts) is only 7.6ms = 19%. The "attention is the fixed half, ceiling ~32" thesis is explicitly retracted in your own doc. This single fact reorders the whole roadmap: every copy-stream / coalesce / sync-tax / prefetch hack attacks the 19-26% non-CPU portion and is Amdahl-capped below 32 t/s no matter how perfect. The ONLY lever that scales to 43+ is making the CPU dequant kernel faster, because it is the 74% term and it is COMPUTE-bound (26% STREAM), so an ISA-width/VNNI win converts almost 1:1 to wall-clock at fixed cores and fixed memory traffic. I verified the premise on the built binary: ggml_vec_dot_q2_K_q8_K and _q3_K are AVX2-only in both source (x86/quants.c:1436/1628, gated #if defined __AVX2__) and the shipped libggml-cpu.so (objdump: q2_K = 148 ymm / 0 zmm / 0 vpdpbusd, q3_K = 269 ymm / 0 zmm), while /proc/cpuinfo confirms full avx512f/bw/vl/vnni/bf16 on the 9965WX. Note Zen5 has NO AMX (empty flags) — kill any AMX idea. Realistic path to 43: AVX-512+VNNI kernels (1.4-1.7x on cmp_cpu) gets you to ~33-36 alone; stack with the now-fixed dual-GPU host-staged copy split (commit d3bf582, lets k drop so more of cmp_cpu moves to GPU under independent dual-link copy) and you clear 43. The kernel win is mandatory and primary; dual-GPU is the multiplier that only pays off once cmp_cpu per-CPU-expert is cheap.

ranked.name: AVX-512 + VNNI Q2_K/Q3_K expert vec_dot kernels (Zen5 full-width)
ranked.why: Attacks cmp_cpu = 74% of decode (29.5ms) — the ONLY term large enough to reach 43. VERIFIED on the built binary, not speculated: both inner loops are AVX2-only (q2_K 148 ymm/0 zmm, q3_K 269 ymm/0 zmm, zero vpdpbusd) yet the CPU has full AVX512F/BW/VL/VNNI/BF16. Because cmp_cpu is COMPUTE-bound (26% STREAM, your own number), fewer instrs/cycle at identical cores and identical memory traffic cuts wall-clock almost 1:1 — the 'cores already saturated' objection does NOT apply to an ISA-width upgrade. Honest magnitude: 512-bit datapath + VNNI vpdpbusd folds the int8 dot, but the Q2_K/Q3_K per-16-element SCALE handling resists vpdpbusd (it gives an unscaled dot; ik_llama restructures into per-scale-group partials), so isolated kernel speedup is ~1.4-1.7x (matches ik_llama's published q2/q3 numbers), NOT 2x. 29.5ms/1.5 = ~20ms cmp_cpu -> ~32-36 t/s standalone. This is the headline because it is the only single hack that moves the dominant term and is de-risked by lifting ik_llama's proven HAVE_FANCY_SIMD kernels verbatim rather than deriving.
ranked.first_experiment: Kernel-only microbench BEFORE touching the server. Standalone harness calling ggml_vec_dot_q2_K_q8_K and _q3_K on the real expert shapes (reduction over d_model=6144, FFN=2048) in a 1-core tight loop. Time AVX2 baseline, then drop ik_llama's __AVX512BW__/__AVX512VNNI__ kernel under a new branch and re-time. ~30 min, zero server risk. Gate correctness by cosine vs AVX2 output (>0.99999; bit-exact impossible due to accumulation reassociation). Decision: only proceed to rebuild if isolated X>=1.4. Then project new cmp_cpu = 29.5/X and re-sweep k (cheaper CPU experts shift the optimum toward MORE-on-CPU, recovering 1-2 t/s second-order).

ranked.name: Fixed dual-GPU host-staged copy split (k-drop multiplier)
ranked.why: The earlier dual-GPU rejection in the dead-list was the BROKEN version (g1 read CUDA0-resident inputs -> P2P serialized on CUDA0 stream, dual_k4=18.29 t/s, WORSE). That is now FIXED in commit d3bf582: g1 reads host-staged inputs (inp_cpu / sel/w slices) so CUDA0 and CUDA1 copy from host on independent PCIe links, truly parallel. This is unmeasured but the theory in the spec (line 276) is sound: GPU-branch copy 18->~9ms across 2 links, overlapping the CPU branch. It does NOT attack the 74% cmp_cpu term directly — its value is that with 2x copy bandwidth you can drop k (move MORE experts off CPU to GPU) without the copy becoming the limiter, which shrinks cmp_cpu. CRUCIAL ORDERING: dual-GPU alone with today's slow CPU kernel still leaves cmp_cpu dominant; it only pays its full ~42 t/s once the per-CPU-expert cost is already low (hack #1) so the rebalance actually helps. Watch the Q3_K mmvq mmid batch threshold (ne[2]<=5): dropping k too far (n_gpu>5) trips use_cuda_graph=false -> the 7-30x MMVQ-miss regime. Keep n_gpu<=5.
ranked.first_experiment: Zero new code — it is already committed. On the persistent server (LLAMA_MOE_CTL) write 'k4 dual' to /tmp/moe_ctl and measure per-token in_gpu/cmp_cpu with GGML_SCHED_SYNC_COUNT=3 vs single k6. Confirm (a) both GPUs show concurrent PCIe in nvidia-smi dmon, (b) in_gpu does NOT regress past single (the d3bf582 fix's whole point), (c) n_gpu stays <=5 so CUDA graph survives. If in_gpu overlaps cleanly, sweep k down with the dual flag and find the new optimum.

ranked.name: Q3_K row-interleaved repack (block_q3_Kx8) + AVX512 gemv for CPU experts
ranked.why: Partially corrects the verdict: I confirmed q3_K has ZERO repack (grep GGML_TYPE_Q3_K repack.cpp = 0) but ALSO that repack.cpp's gemv DOES have AVX512 paths (2260 _mm512 intrinsics, gated #if defined __AVX512F__ at lines 29/101/116) — so a new block_q3_Kx8 gemv would get 512-bit FOR FREE, contradicting the verdict's 'both paths AVX2' claim. Still secondary because: (a) it also only attacks cmp_cpu, redundant in mechanism with #1 (both speed the same dequant), and (b) it requires routing CPU experts through cpu_repack buffer instead of -ot CUDA_Host, plus a pinned bounce buffer for the GPU branch — real plumbing that risks breaking the working overlap. Genuine extra value over #1: covers down-proj Q3_K with contiguous 8-col streaming (helps the 26%-STREAM symptom via access pattern, complementary to vec_dot widening) AND q3_K is the one type with no fast path at all. If #1's microbench shows the scale-path resists VNNI on q3_K specifically, this repack is the fallback that gets q3_K its win. Do NOT do both q3_K paths; pick whichever microbenches faster.
ranked.first_experiment: Zero-code first cut: temporarily drop -ot CUDA_Host for ONE offloaded layer's gate/up tensors (q2_K, which already has an 8x8 repack path) so they land in cpu_repack, measure per-token ms delta on that layer. This tests 'does the repack buffer beat the CUDA_Host vec_dot path' with no new code. If the q2_K repack path already beats vec_dot by >15%, build block_q3_Kx8 + AVX512 gemv for the down-proj. If <8%, the access-pattern win is absorbed by compute-bound dequant and this is not worth the plumbing.

ranked.name: Drop dead DSA indexer tensors from VRAM (free a fraction of a resident layer)
ranked.why: VERIFIED CORRECT and free: glm-dsa creates indexer_k_norm/proj/attn_k/attn_q_b (glm-dsa.cpp:107-111) but glm-dsa reuses deepseek2::graph (models.h:1013) which NEVER reads them — grep for member access (.indexer_ / ->indexer_) across all of src returns ZERO graph reads (only struct decls in llama-model.h and the create_tensor calls). The DSA sparse-selection indexer is loaded but not wired into the forward graph; it falls back to dense MLA, so these ~0.83GB are pure dead VRAM. Freeing them lets ~0.28 of a MoE layer become resident -> ~1.5% fewer host-read bytes/token -> ~0.3 t/s. Tiny, but byte-identical, S-effort, and it is the ONLY part of the coalesce-merge candidate that is real (the gate/up/down contiguous-H2D half is false: 3 separate tensors with distinct Q2_K/Q3_K block sizes, needs weight repack + matmul fusion, and the copy is bandwidth-bound not launch-bound so coalescing saves nothing). Bank it as headroom for #2's k-drop.
ranked.first_experiment: Add TENSOR_SKIP|TENSOR_NOT_REQUIRED to the 5 indexer create_tensor calls at glm-dsa.cpp:107-111, rebuild, run llama-bench decode batch=1. Verify perplexity is byte-identical (must be — never read) and nvidia-smi shows ~0.83 GB more free. Then feed that headroom into #2's resident-layer packing.

ranked.name: -ub 64 prefill-buffer shrink + correct phantom MLA KV budget
ranked.why: glm-dsa is MLA (has_v=false, single latent K head) so the project's '8GB KV余裕' is ~20x too large — real KV ~0.37GB at ctx=4096. Default -ub 512 permanently reserves a 512-token prefill compute graph that is dead VRAM at batch=1. Setting -b 256 -ub 64 plus recomputing the -ot resident packing against the true KV size may free enough to make ONE more MoE layer resident = one fewer offloaded layer's cmp_cpu every token (~0.5-0.9ms). Honestly capped LOW (+1-1.5 t/s) because the reclaimable GB may only buy a fraction of a layer and the phantom-8GB may be a planning fudge not a real allocation. But S-effort, byte-identical, stacks additively with everything. Do NOT pursue -ctk/-ctv KV-quant — near-zero on MLA's single tiny latent head.
ranked.first_experiment: Run server with -b 256 -ub 64 and the recomputed -ot map (one more layer resident), confirm via nvidia-smi it fits without OOM and decode t/s rises. If it fits, also test whether the freed GB instead enables #2's k-drop better than an extra resident layer — compare the two uses of the same headroom.

ranked.name: Dedicated copy-stream / coalesce / async-join / sync-tax cleanups
ranked.why: All of these (copy-stream, contiguous H2D, Q8-input-once, async pinned join, kill per-layer syncs) target the in_gpu + cross-backend portion = 7.6ms = 19% of decode, and most of that is JOIN-WAIT on the CPU branch, not the copy. Your own spec retracts the timings these rely on: the GPU-branch copy at optimal k6 is sub-1ms (2 experts x 11.5MB / 57GB/s), part_cpu is plain malloc (unpinned, so cudaMemcpyAsync silently serializes), Q2_K/Q3_K already declare vec_dot_type=Q8_K (input is quantized once already), and 'events at n_copies=1' already proved sync-count down but wall-clock flat. Even perfect execution of all five leaves >74% of the token (cmp_cpu) untouched -> hard-capped below 32 t/s, cannot reach 43. Skip unless a microbench surprises.
ranked.first_experiment: Refutation, not implementation: on the warm k6 server flip LLAMA_MOE_CTL to k=8 (all-CPU = zero GPU-branch H2D) vs k=6 and compare in_gpu via GGML_SCHED_SYNC_COUNT=3. If in_gpu(k8) ~= in_gpu(k6), the copy was never on the critical path and the whole class is confirmed dead. ~2 min, no rebuild.

ranked.name: Re-enable CUDA kernel fusion in 2-GPU config
ranked.why: VERIFIED dead by code read. The real operator fusion (MUL_MAT_ID+GLU at can_fuse:3603, topk-MoE at 3392) runs in ggml_cuda_try_fuse (3824), called UNCONDITIONALLY per device at 4369, gated ONLY by GGML_CUDA_DISABLE_FUSION (default enabled) — NOT by device count. The device-count-gated function at 4550-4562 (ggml_backend_cuda_graph_optimize) is a separate stream-concurrency pass that needs a 3-way attn_norm QKV fork, which GLM-DSA's MLA (KV head 1, compressed KV) does not present. So SwiGLU/MoE/RMS fusions are already ON for both GPUs, and the concurrency pass would no-op on this arch anyway. Nothing to unlock.
ranked.first_experiment: 5-min confirmation: run decode with GGML_CUDA_DISABLE_FUSION=1 vs without. If t/s is essentially unchanged, fusion is already on and saturated; verdict confirmed.

ranked.name: Cross-layer router-predicted GPU-branch expert prefetch
ranked.why: Structurally weak for THIS arch. L+1's router input is norm(attn_{L+1}(L_full_output)) where L's moe_out is exactly what you're still computing — so you can only run a proxy router on L's STALE pre-MoE residual, not the real L+1 router early. Claimed 90-97% recall is ungrounded and your own measurement (uniform routing, cover80~160, NO cross-token reuse) is direct evidence AGAINST strong adjacent-layer correlation. Even at perfect recall it only hides the sub-1ms copy (already off critical path per #6), touches zero of the 74% cmp_cpu, and dropping k to widen the GPU share trips the Q3_K mmvq ne[2]<=5 threshold -> CUDA graph disable -> 7-30x regression. High code cost, near-zero expected payoff.
ranked.first_experiment: Free offline gate before any code: dump real top-8 expert ids per token/layer (GLM_DUMP_EXPERTS logger already exists, cli.cpp:447) over ~500 decode tokens. Python: for each adjacent MoE-layer pair compute recall of a top-12-superset predictor vs L+1's actual top-8. GATE: if superset recall <90%, abort. Given uniform routing, expect failure — refutes at near-zero cost.

roadmap.do_first: Hack #1 — AVX-512 + VNNI Q2_K/Q3_K vec_dot kernels — and START with the isolated kernel microbenchmark, NOT a server rebuild. This is the highest-leverage move because cmp_cpu (CPU expert dequant) is the verified 74% of decode (29.5ms of ~40ms, per CPU_GPU_SPLIT_SPEC.md:285) and it is compute-bound (26% STREAM), so it is the ONLY term whose improvement scales to 43+; every other hack attacks the 19-26% remainder and is Amdahl-capped below 32. The premise is already proven true on YOUR built binary: objdump of libggml-cpu.so shows ggml_vec_dot_q2_K_q8_K = 148 ymm / 0 zmm and ggml_vec_dot_q3_K_q8_K = 269 ymm / 0 zmm with zero vpdpbusd, while the 9965WX has full AVX512F/BW/VL/VNNI/BF16 (and NO AMX — ignore AMX). First experiment, ~30 min, zero server risk: write a standalone harness that calls both vec_dot functions on the real expert shapes (reduction over d_model=6144, FFN=2048) in a single-core tight loop, time the AVX2 baseline, then lift ik_llama's proven HAVE_FANCY_SIMD __AVX512BW__/__AVX512VNNI__ kernels verbatim under a new branch and re-time on identical shapes. Gate correctness by cosine similarity vs AVX2 output (expect >0.99999; bit-exact is impossible due to accumulation reassociation). DECISION RULE: only rebuild/redeploy the full server if the isolated kernel speedup X >= 1.4. If X clears the bar, project new cmp_cpu = 29.5ms/X and re-sweep k (cheaper CPU experts shift the optimum toward more-on-CPU), then layer the now-fixed dual-GPU host-staged split (commit d3bf582) on top to clear 43.

shortlist.name: AVX-512 + VNNI Q2_K/Q3_K expert vec_dot kernels (Zen5 full-width)
shortlist.one_line: Add #if defined(__AVX512F__)/__AVX512VNNI__ branches to ggml_vec_dot_q2_K_q8_K (quants.c:1436) and ggml_vec_dot_q3_K_q8_K (:1628), porting ik_llama's proven Zen4/5 kernels: 512-bit unpack + _mm512_dpbusd_epi32 fused int8 dot, halving the dominant CPU-dequant kernel's instruction count.
shortlist.rationale: MERGES raw hacks #8(AVX512 q2/q3 vec_dot), #28(VNNI variant), #34, #43(dequant-once-ram pivot to kernel). VALIDATED IN CODE: both vec_dot bodies are literally `#if defined __AVX2__` with zero __m512 (confirmed by sed of lines 1448/1644), while /proc/cpuinfo confirms avx512f/bw/vl/vnni/bf16 and the file already ships an AVX512VNNI helper at line 106. Attacks cmp_cpu (~20ms, the largest single decode term, spec-confirmed COMPUTE-bound at 26% STREAM — so fewer instrs/cycle directly cuts wall-clock at identical core count and memory traffic). The 'capped because 24 cores saturated' objection is wrong for an ISA-width upgrade. De-risked by lifting ik_llama's HAVE_FANCY_SIMD kernels rather than deriving. Bit-identity gate mandatory (accumulation-order ULP risk). Highest-confidence large lever; this is the headline.

shortlist.name: Q3_K row-interleaved repack (q3_K_8x8) + AVX-512 gemv, CPU experts in repack buffer
shortlist.one_line: Add the missing block_q3_Kx8 repack + AVX512 gemv (none exists today — grep GGML_TYPE_Q3_K repack.cpp = 0) AND place CPU-branch offloaded experts in the cpu_repack buffer (not CUDA_Host) so gate/up Q2_K already get the 8x8 interleaved gemv and down Q3_K finally does too, with a small pinned bounce buffer feeding the GPU branch.
shortlist.rationale: MERGES #9(Q3_Kx8 repack), #26(route CPU experts through existing q2_K_8x8 repack), #29(VNNI repack), #30(Q3->Q4 promote alt). VALIDATED: repack.cpp ships q2_K/q4_K/q5_K_8x8 gemv but ZERO Q3_K (grep confirmed); the fast path is gated on cpu_repack_buffer_type (line 4778/4794) which -ot=CUDA_Host bypasses, so ALL CPU experts run the slow per-row scalar path today. The 8x8 interleave amortizes activation re-quant across 8 columns and streams contiguously (fixes the 26%-STREAM symptom via access pattern — a complementary mechanism to vec_dot widening) and covers the previously-uncovered down Q3_K. Stacks with the vec_dot hack. Real risk: repack-buffer vs CUDA_Host coexistence for the GPU bounce copy needs careful plumbing (parallel tensor alloc), and the bounce reintroduces a per-token pinned copy — must verify the CPU∥GPU overlap survives. Lossless layout repack, bit-near-identical.

shortlist.name: Re-enable CUDA kernel fusion (MUL_MAT_ID+GLU, attn) in the 2-GPU config
shortlist.one_line: Patch ggml_backend_cuda_graph_optimize (ggml-cuda.cu:4538) so fusion runs per-device instead of early-returning whenever get_device_count()!=1, and flip the default-off GGML_CUDA_GRAPH_OPT gate — unlocking MoE/attention kernel fusions currently globally disabled for the entire forward pass.
shortlist.rationale: MERGES #47(single-GPU fusion re-enable) and #48(split-count reduction). VALIDATED by reading the function body: `if (!enable_graph_optimization) return;` (default OFF) AND `if (!use_cuda_graph || get_device_count()!=1) return;`. The harness always runs CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0,1, so fusion of MUL_MAT_ID+GLU and attention sites (ops at 3618-3660) is OFF for the WHOLE token. Attacks the FIXED GPU-work half (~19ms) the prompt flags as the biggest uncapped target — fusion removes a kernel launch + an HBM round-trip for the SwiGLU intermediate per resident MoE layer (~57) + attention QKV/o fusions (79 layers). FREE first step: run GGML_CUDA_GRAPH_OPT=1 and confirm it early-returns. Risk: per-device rescoping interacts with the split's cross-device copy nodes; guard behind a new env, byte-identity gate on GLM-4.7-Flash proxy first. Medium effort, attacks fixed cost, not capped to the offloaded half.

shortlist.name: Cross-layer router-predicted GPU-branch expert prefetch (double-buffered ring)
shortlist.one_line: Run layer L+1's router on layer L's residual (predict top-12 superset, ~90-97% recall), async-prefetch the GPU-branch experts H2D into a fixed-address 2-slot VRAM ring during L's 10.5ms CPU-dequant window on a dedicated copy stream; the real L+1 router still drives mul_mat_id with synchronous fallback for the ~3-10% miss.
shortlist.rationale: MERGES #1(cross-layer prediction), #4(double-buffer ring infra), #17(dedicated copy stream), #27(gating-predicted prefetch to rebalance k). The ONE legitimate way around the strict L->L+1 dependency NOT on the dead list — the dead-list rejected naive prefetch of EXACT future IDs, but probabilistic-superset prediction was never tried. Attacks the 18ms copy-bound in_gpu term by hiding the H2D under the prior layer's dead copy-engine time; once copy is hidden the GPU branch becomes compute-bound (~0.6ms/expert) so k drops 6->2-4, also shrinking the 20ms CPU branch. Correct-by-construction: prefetch is a HINT, real router is truth. MANDATORY cheap offline gate FIRST: measure adjacent-layer top-8 overlap on the existing expert_ids log — if recall <90% for THIS DSA-routed arch (indexer top_k=2048 makes adjacency-similarity unverified), abort. Fixed-address ring preserves CUDA-graph reuse. Higher ceiling but more code and prediction-quality risk; ranked below the kernel wins.

shortlist.name: Dedicated copy-engine stream for GPU-branch expert H2D
shortlist.one_line: Add a cudaStreamNonBlocking copy stream to the CUDA backend and issue the offloaded-expert H2D on it (today it shares cudaStreamPerThread with MMVQ, so the ~8ms copy serializes against the ~6ms resident-layer GPU compute it could hide under), gated by an event before the GPU-branch mul_mat_id.
shortlist.rationale: MERGES #17 (dedicated copy stream) as a STANDALONE win and de-risks/enables #4. Blackwell has 2 copy engines separate from SMs; today the GPU-branch copy and the resident-layer GPU compute of the same token serialize on one stream so the copy is fully exposed in the 18ms in_gpu term. Overlapping it under the otherwise-idle resident GPU compute removes most of the copy from the critical path, letting k drop from 6 toward 4-5. Lower effort than full prediction (no router-prediction correctness risk), self-contained, attacks the copy-bound term directly. Risk: the ggml event/WAR machinery assumes single-stream ordering — the second stream needs its own cudaStreamWaitEvent into the compute stream or it races MMVQ. Valuable independently and as scaffolding for the prediction hack.

shortlist.name: Q8-input-once + true async pinned join/prestage copies (kill per-layer sync tax)
shortlist.one_line: Quantize the MoE input to q8 once and share it across gate/up/down (halving from_float + shrinking the prestage from 24KB f32 to 6KB int8), AND replace the join's sync-both-then-blocking-copy (ggml-backend.cpp:1687) with a real cudaMemcpyAsync H2D since part_cpu is CUDA_Host-pinned, skipping the redundant double host-sync.
shortlist.rationale: MERGES #14(async join copy), #15(kill moe_ids GPU readback), #16(pack 3 prestage inputs), #31(prestage decouple), #33(Q8-input-once). Attacks cross-backend overhead + the 5ms in_cpu prestage + the per-layer host-blocking syncs (SYNC_COUNT showed 194-533 GPU-flushes/token). Each removed sync flushes a GPU stream and re-serializes issue across ~19 offloaded layers. Bounded fixed-overhead cleanups (single-digit t/s) but cheap, stack cleanly, and grow PROPORTIONALLY larger once the copy is halved by the stream/prefetch hacks (each sync is a bigger fraction of a shorter critical path). Must LOUD-assert CUDA_Host-pinned before the async path (pageable fallback silently serializes AND becomes a correctness hazard). Gate behind LLAMA_MOE_CPU_SPLIT for byte-identity off-path. Sequence AFTER the copy-stream work lands, then re-measure residual sync tax.

shortlist.name: Coalesce gate+up+down into one contiguous H2D + drop dead indexer weight from VRAM
shortlist.one_line: Pre-pack each offloaded expert's gate/up/down into one contiguous pinned region at load so copy_experts fires one ~11.5MB transfer instead of 3 scattered sub-4MB ones (~114 launches/token -> ~38, better PCIe fill), and move the unreferenced DSA indexer tensors (~0.83GB, confirmed zero graph refs) off the VRAM hot budget.
shortlist.rationale: MERGES #18(coalesce 3 expert tensors per H2D) and #12(drop unused indexer from resident set). VALIDATED: glm-dsa loads indexer.attn_q_b/attn_k/proj (glm-dsa.cpp:106-111) but grep across deepseek2.cpp AND llama-graph.cpp returns ZERO indexer references — pure dead VRAM. build_moe_ffn issues 3 separate MUL_MAT_ID so copy_experts scatters small underfilled copies; one contiguous transfer per used-expert run cuts launch overhead and raises achieved PCIe utilization toward 57GB/s, compounding with overlap (bigger contiguous copies hide more easily). Freeing 0.83GB lets ~71 more experts stay resident (fraction of a layer). Both correct-by-construction (placement/layout only, byte-identical). Lower individual ceiling but cheap, safe, and removes structural inefficiency that bottlenecks the copy-overlap hacks above.

shortlist.name: -ub 64 prefill-buffer shrink + correct the wrong non-MLA KV budget to free a resident layer
shortlist.one_line: Set -b 256 -ub 64 to shrink the permanent prefill compute-buffer reserve (dead VRAM at batch=1), and recompute the resident-layer -ot packing against the TRUE MLA KV size (~0.37GB f16 at ctx=4096, not the spec's phantom 8GB) — together freeing enough VRAM to make one more MoE layer resident (one fewer offloaded layer per token).
shortlist.rationale: MERGES #20(ub-driven compute-buffer reclaim) and #21(MLA KV budget correction; explicitly NOT KV-quant). VALIDATED: split is n_tokens==1 (decode), and glm-dsa is MLA (has_v=false, single latent K head) so the project's '8GB KV余裕' budget is ~20x too large — KV is ~0.37GB at ctx=4096. The harness doesn't override -ub (default 512 reserves a 512-token prefill graph permanently). Each freed ~3GB resident layer removes that layer's CPU expert work from every token (~0.5-0.9ms after overlap). Cheapest hack on the list (config + -ot remap, no code), attacks host-read-volume by reducing the offloaded layer count at the CPU=19/CPU=18-OOM edge. Honestly CAPPED LOW (~+1-1.5 t/s) — reclaimable GB may only buy a fraction of a layer and the phantom-8GB may be a planning fudge not a real allocation — but S-effort, safe, byte-identical, worth banking. Do NOT pursue -ctk/-ctv KV-quant (near-zero on MLA).

verdicts.name: AVX-512 + VNNI Q2_K/Q3_K expert vec_dot kernels (Zen5 full-width)
verdicts.blocking_issues: No hard blocker; no OOM (pure compute, zero new allocation). The premise is EMPIRICALLY TRUE on the built binary, not just speculated: build is -march=native on Zen5, so __AVX512F/BW/VL/VNNI/BF16__ and __AVXVNNI__ are all compile-defined, YET the active q2_K/q3_K kernels (arch/x86/quants.c:1448/1644, NOT root quants.c:1436 as the proposal mis-cited) are gated #if defined __AVX2__ and use only 256-bit intrinsics. objdump of libggml-cpu.so confirms: q2_K = 64 ymm / 0 zmm, q3_K = 116 ymm / 0 zmm. VNNI vpdpbusd is used in 681 sites elsewhere but ZERO in these two inner loops (they use vpmaddubsw + vpmaddwd). nrc==1 single-row path is the registered vec_dot for all MoE expert matmuls (ggml-cpu.c:291), no repacked GEMM bypass. So the lever exists. The real issue is magnitude, not feasibility. The 35 t/s headline overstates by assuming (a) 2x instruction halving and (b) full translation to wall-clock. Reality: Q2_K/Q3_K inner loops are dominated by per-16-element sub-block SCALE handling (8 maddubs + 9 madd_epi16 per block), which VNNI's vpdpbusd cannot fold (dpbusd gives an unscaled dot; ik_llama restructures into per-scale-group partials, real but not drop-in 50%). Width 256->512 on Zen5's native 512-bit datapath is genuine 1-cycle but the loop is a dependent-accumulate chain bounded by load ports + latency, so kernel speedup is ~1.3-1.6x (matches ik_llama's reported q2_K/q3_K numbers), not 2x.
verdicts.what_would_kill_it: Amdahl against the already-working k=6 overlap. The kernel only touches the CPU-expert ~20 ms of the 42 ms baseline; the other ~19 ms (GPU attention+resident across all 79 layers + cross-backend sync) is untouched. CRUCIALLY the current best (27 t/s, 37 ms) is NOT the un-overlapped baseline: the CPU expert branch already runs CONCURRENT with the GPU 2-expert branch per offloaded layer. So a 1.4x CPU-kernel speedup is partially ABSORBED by the existing overlap floor — once the CPU branch drops below the per-layer GPU branch (~13-15 ms), the GPU branch becomes the limiter and further CPU speedup buys nothing on that layer. If the measured CPU-expert term is actually more bandwidth-shadowed than compute (the brief says 26% STREAM = compute-bound, which I take at face value, but if even part of the 20 ms is the cross-backend copy/sync rather than the vec_dot itself, the kernel win shrinks further). If the isolated-kernel speedup comes in under ~1.3x (plausible because the scale path resists VNNI), net wall-clock save is ~2-3 ms -> ~28-29 t/s, i.e. barely above current 27.
verdicts.first_experiment: Kernel-only microbenchmark BEFORE touching the model. Write a standalone harness that calls ggml_vec_dot_q2_K_q8_K and _q3_K on the real expert shapes (n = 2048 for gate/up reduction over d_model=6144 tiles, and the down proj) in a tight loop on 1 core, time it. Then drop in ik_llama's HAVE_FANCY_SIMD 512-bit kernel under a new #if defined(__AVX512BW__) branch (lift verbatim, it's proven) and time again on the same shapes. This gives the true isolated kernel speedup (X) in ~30 min with zero risk to the working server. Gate correctness with cosine similarity vs the AVX2 output (expect >0.99999; bit-exact is impossible due to accumulation reassociation). THEN project: new CPU-branch time = 20ms/X, and check against the GPU-branch overlap floor — only if X>=1.4 AND the CPU branch stays above the GPU branch does it convert to t/s. If X clears the bar, re-sweep k (cheaper experts may shift optimum toward more-on-CPU, recovering ~1-2 t/s second-order). Do NOT rebuild/redeploy the full server until the microbench shows X>=1.4.

verdicts.name: Q3_K row-interleaved repack (q3_K_8x8) + AVX-512 gemv, CPU experts in repack buffer
verdicts.blocking_issues: The proposal's central premise is partly false, verified by reading the source. (1) Its claim that "ALL CPU experts run the slow per-row SCALAR path today" is wrong: with -ot CUDA_Host the experts run the standard ggml.c MUL_MAT_ID path, which calls ggml_vec_dot_q2_K_q8_K and ggml_vec_dot_q3_K_q8_K — and BOTH are already AVX2-vectorized (x86/quants.c:1448 for q2_K, :1644 for q3_K). The baseline is not scalar, it is AVX2. (2) Its claim that the 8x8 interleave is "a complementary mechanism to vec_dot widening" is also wrong: the repack q2_K/q4_K 8x8 gemv in x86/repack.cpp is AVX2 only (_mm512 count = 0, _mm256 count = 220 in the q2_K/q4_K 8x8 region; the gemv body is gated on __AVX2__ at line 1731, NOT __AVX512F__). So there is no SIMD-width gain — both old and new paths are 256-bit. (3) The activation->q8_K requant-amortization it cites as a win is largely already present: standard mul_mat requants the activation to q8_K once per token and reuses it across all rows. WHAT IS TRUE: q3_K genuinely has zero repack/8x8 (grep GGML_TYPE_Q3_K repack.cpp = 0, confirmed), so implementing block_q3_Kx8 + gemv is real new work. The genuine benefit reduces to (a) contiguous 8-column-interleaved memory streaming (classic repack benefit, historically ~5-20% on K-quant gemv) and (b) extending that to down-proj Q3_K (~1/3 of expert FLOPs). No OOM from repack itself: get_alloc_size=nullptr defaults to ggml_nbytes and block_q2_Kx8 is exactly 8x block_q2_K (lossless layout), so block_q3_Kx8 would be 8x block_q3_K with no inflation. The flagged OOM risk is only the CUDA_Host bounce-buffer coexistence (plumbing, not a hard blocker). Correctness is fine: lossless repack. Caps: the hack only touches the CPU-expert compute (~20ms of the 42ms baseline / ~18ms of the 37ms current critical path), and only the fraction that survives the k=6 CPU||GPU split. Cost model: even an optimistic 20% speedup on the CPU branch yields ~30 t/s; realistic 10-15% yields ~28-29 t/s. The est_tps_after=38 requires a ~50%+ CPU-branch speedup that is implausible when both paths are AVX2. It does NOT touch the ~half-of-decode fixed GPU work (attention 79 layers + resident experts), the cross-backend sync, or the per-token host-read volume — the targets the brief says hold the biggest wins.
verdicts.what_would_kill_it: A microbenchmark showing the existing AVX2 q3_K vec_dot (current down-proj path) already saturates the same effective throughput as a q3_Kx8 gemv would — i.e. the 8x8 interleave buys <8% on this specific d_model=6144/FFN=2048 expert shape because the data already fits L2 and the AVX2 vec_dot is compute-bound on the same dequant ALU ops, not memory-streaming-bound. Since the brief states CPU is COMPUTE-bound (only 26% STREAM bw), the repack's access-pattern improvement attacks a bandwidth symptom that is NOT the binding constraint — the dequant ALU work (q3_K hi/lo bit unpack, scale application) is identical per element regardless of layout. That makes the realistic gain land near the bottom of the 5-20% range, i.e. ~28 t/s, killing the 38 claim. Also fatal: if writing a true AVX512 (_mm512) q2_K/q3_K gemv is what's actually needed for a real win, that is a much larger, separate effort than "merge the missing repack" and is not what this proposal delivers.
verdicts.first_experiment: Do NOT build the repack first. Instead run a 30-minute isolation microbench that bounds the ceiling before any plumbing: write a standalone harness that times (a) the current ggml_vec_dot_q3_K_q8_K AVX2 path vs (b) a hand-rolled or existing q4_K_8x8-style interleaved gemv, both on a synthetic [6144 x 2048] q3_K weight x q8_K activation, single-threaded, 10k iters, on this exact 9965WX. If the interleaved gemv is <1.15x the vec_dot on q3_K shapes, the whole proposal caps at ~28-29 t/s and is not worth the plumbing. If it is >1.3x, proceed. Cheaper still as a true zero-code first cut: temporarily drop -ot CUDA_Host for ONE offloaded layer's gate/up tensors so they land in the cpu_repack buffer (gate/up are q2_K, which DOES have an 8x8 path already) and measure per-token ms delta on that layer — this directly tests "does routing CPU experts through the existing repack buffer beat the CUDA_Host vec_dot path" using zero new code, and isolates the q3_K-specific work from the buffer-routing question.

verdicts.name: Re-enable CUDA kernel fusion (MUL_MAT_ID+GLU, attn) in the 2-GPU config
verdicts.blocking_issues: Premise is factually wrong, verified by reading the code. (1) The targeted function ggml_backend_cuda_graph_optimize (ggml-cuda.cu:4538) does NOT perform MUL_MAT_ID+GLU or attention operator fusion — it is a QKV stream-concurrency/interleaving pass that builds concurrent_events/stream_mapping (4700-4776). (2) The REAL fusion (MUL_MAT_ID+GLU at ggml_cuda_can_fuse:3603, topk-MoE at ggml_cuda_topk_moe_fusion:3392) runs in ggml_cuda_try_fuse (3824), called at 4369 inside ggml_cuda_graph_evaluate_and_capture, which executes UNCONDITIONALLY per device (4508) and is gated ONLY by GGML_CUDA_DISABLE_FUSION (default = enabled), NOT by get_device_count(). So the SwiGLU/MoE/RMS-norm fusions on resident experts are ALREADY ON for both GPUs in the 2-GPU config. There is no globally-disabled fusion to unlock; the hack's central claim ('fusion is OFF for the WHOLE token') is false. (3) Even the concurrency pass it would enable requires fan-out==3 on a node named 'attn_norm' (4616-4628); GLM-DSA uses MLA (KV head 1, compressed KV) which does not present the classic 3-way attn_norm QKV fork, so the pass likely never fires for this arch. (4) At batch=1 decode, QKV GEMVs are tiny launch-latency-bound ops; multi-stream concurrency yields marginal benefit and touches only a sub-fraction of the ~19ms fixed GPU half — it does not touch the offloaded-expert CPU dequant half (~20ms) at all.
verdicts.what_would_kill_it: It is already killed by the code read: the fusion it claims to unlock is not in that function and is already enabled elsewhere. Empirically, GGML_CUDA_DISABLE_FUSION=1 re-bench showing near-zero t/s drop would confirm fusion contributes little; and GGML_CUDA_GRAPH_OPT=1 producing no 'Adding stream at node ...attn_norm' debug log on the GLM-DSA MLA graph would confirm the concurrency pass is a no-op for this architecture, leaving the per-device patch with nothing to do.
verdicts.first_experiment: Free, 5 minutes, no code change: run the existing decode bench twice — once with GGML_CUDA_DISABLE_FUSION=1 and once without. If t/s is essentially unchanged, fusion is already on and saturated, so 're-enabling' it cannot help (verdict confirmed dead). Separately, on a single-GPU run set GGML_CUDA_GRAPH_OPT=1 with GGML_LOG_DEBUG and grep stderr for 'Adding stream at node'; if it never prints an attn_norm fork for the GLM-DSA graph, the concurrency pass does not apply to MLA and the 2-GPU patch is moot.

verdicts.name: Cross-layer router-predicted GPU-branch expert prefetch (double-buffered ring)
verdicts.blocking_issues: 1) Prediction premise is structurally off: deepseek2/glm-dsa dataflow (verified, src/models/glm4-moe.cpp:210-263 + models.h:1013 using deepseek2::graph) is L+1_router_input = norm(attn_{L+1}(L_full_output)). L+1 attention input IS L's moe_out, which you're still computing — so you cannot run "L+1's real router early," only a proxy on L's STALE pre-MoE residual. Claimed 90-97% recall is ungrounded; the prompt's own measurement (uniform routing, cover80~160, no cross-token reuse) is evidence AGAINST strong adjacent-layer top-8 correlation. 2) Fixed-cost ceiling: branches ALREADY overlap (ggml-backend.cpp build_branch pins CPU vs GPU concurrently). Wall ~= max(CPU~20ms, GPU~18ms copy) + overhead, not sum. Hiding the 18ms H2D under the PRIOR layer's 10.5ms CPU window hides at most ~58% (10.5<18). Once GPU stops binding, the CPU branch (~20ms) + untouched attention/resident GPU work set the wall — prefetch touches neither. 3) CIRCULARITY: shrinking k needs the GPU branch to absorb MORE experts, which RE-GROWS the very copy you tried to hide. 4) NEWLY FOUND CUDA-graph killer: get_mmvq_mmid_max_batch_turing_plus (mmvq.cu:140) caps Q3_K down-proj at ne[2]<=5. k=6 (n_gpu=2) ok; k->2-4 (n_gpu=4-6) exceeds 5 -> use_cuda_graph=false (ggml-cuda.cu:3261-3273) -> slow stream-sync MMID path = the 7-30x class already on the dead list. "Fixed-address ring preserves graph reuse" is moot; graph dies from batch threshold, not address.
verdicts.what_would_kill_it: The offline recall gate failing (or coming back marginal): adjacent-layer top-8 overlap <90% for the predicted superset. Given uniform routing (cover80~160, no cross-token reuse) this is the likely outcome — and it kills the whole idea before any code. Secondary kill: even at 100% recall the gain is capped at partial hiding of the ~18ms copy (~6-8ms) while CPU branch ~20ms + fixed GPU attention/resident work (the ~half-of-decode this does NOT touch) become binding; net stays near current k=6 result. Tertiary kill: dropping k to widen the GPU share trips the Q3_K ne[2]<=5 CUDA-graph threshold -> sync-path regression that can net-NEGATIVE the token rate.
verdicts.first_experiment: Zero new code — the fork already has the logger. Run llama-cli with GLM_DUMP_EXPERTS=/mnt/optane/experts.jsonl on ~500 decode tokens (the cb_eval at cli.cpp:447 dumps per-token per-layer ffn_moe_topk = the real selected top-8 ids). Then a ~20-line python: for each token, for each adjacent MoE-layer pair (L, L+1), compute recall = |topk(L) ∩ topk(L+1)| / 8, and also test a SUPERSET predictor (does the union of L's top-12 by router-logit cover L+1's top-8?). Report mean recall and the fraction of (token,layer) pairs with recall>=7/8. GATE: if superset recall <90%, abort — the prediction is too weak for this DSA arch and the prefetch is a HINT that misses too often, forcing the synchronous-fallback re-copy (re-introducing the sync the 'events at n_copies=1' work already showed isn't the bottleneck). Given uniform routing, expect this gate to fail or be marginal, which refutes the hack at near-zero cost.

verdicts.name: Dedicated copy-engine stream for GPU-branch expert H2D
verdicts.blocking_issues: 1) Stale premise: the hack anchors on "18ms in_gpu" and "~6ms idle resident GPU compute" — but CPU_GPU_SPLIT_SPEC.md L281-290 explicitly RETRACTS those. Clean server-warmed timing: in_gpu=7.6ms (19% of decode; per the SYNC_TIME instrumentation it is host-blocked input/JOIN-WAIT time, NOT GPU compute), cmp_gpu=1.8-2.8ms (async graph replay, ~0). The "~6ms idle resident GPU compute to hide the copy under" does not exist. 2) Wrong target: real bottleneck is cmp_cpu = CPU Q2_K/Q3_K dequant = 29.5ms = 74% of decode, host-blocking, untouched by any GPU-side stream change. 3) Copy is tiny at the optimal config: at best k=6 only 2 experts go to GPU = 23MB / 57GB/s ~= 0.4ms (spec est 0.2ms/expert). The "8ms copy" is the k=4 number; at k=6 it is sub-1ms. Removing it fully saves <1ms on a 39ms token. 4) "Let k drop 6->4-5" is backwards: measured k-sweep is monotonic toward FEWER GPU experts (k4=0.995x, k5=1.04x, k6=1.14x) because the GPU branch is single-PCIe-link copy-bound. 5) CUDA graphs are ENABLED for the GPU branch in decode (Blackwell mmvq mmid_max Q3_K=5 >= ne[2]=n_gpu=2, ggml-cuda.cu:3261-3273); copy_experts H2D is issued OUTSIDE the captured graph. Event-gating a captured-graph MUL_MAT_ID against a separate stream is >> M effort and risks graph-disable -> 7-30x MMVQ-miss regime (already dead-listed). 6) The fork ALREADY has full intra-graph multi-stream fork/join machinery (ggml_cuda_concurrent_event, GGML_CUDA_MAX_STREAMS=8, ggml-cuda.cu:4234-4348); prior author still found cmp_cpu, not the copy, was the bottleneck. No OOM and correctness is fixable, but irrelevant given the target term is ~1ms and already overlapped.
verdicts.what_would_kill_it: Already killed by the measured breakdown: cmp_cpu (CPU dequant) is 74% of decode and host-blocking; in_gpu is only 19% and is dominated by the join-wait on the CPU branch, not the copy; the GPU-branch copy at the optimal k=6 is sub-1ms. Any change that only overlaps the GPU-branch H2D leaves >97% of the token untouched. Definitive kill test: setting k=8 (all-CPU, zero GPU-branch copy) leaves in_gpu essentially unchanged vs k6, proving the copy was never on the critical path. Also CUDA-graph capture of the GPU branch means resident compute is async graph replay (cmp_gpu ~2ms total) already overlapping the CPU branch, so the hack's "copy serializes against ~6ms resident compute on one stream" picture does not hold.
verdicts.first_experiment: Zero code. On the current k6 build run one server-warmed decode with GGML_SCHED_SYNC_COUNT=3 and record per-token in_gpu/cmp_cpu/cmp_gpu/in_cpu; then on the same warm server flip LLAMA_MOE_CTL to k=8 (all-CPU = zero GPU-branch H2D) vs k=6 and compare in_gpu. If in_gpu(k8) ~= in_gpu(k6), the copy is not on the critical path and the dedicated-copy-stream hack cannot help -> abandon. ~2 min, no rebuild. The real lever is the 74% cmp_cpu term: faster Q2_K/Q3_K CPU dequant (Zen5 AVX512-VNNI/AMX) or pre-dequant of offloaded experts.

verdicts.name: Q8-input-once + true async pinned join/prestage copies (kill per-layer sync tax)
verdicts.blocking_issues: Every sub-component is bounded to noise against the 37ms/token critical path. (1) Q8-input-once headline is moot: verified in ggml-cpu.c:289-298 that Q2_K and Q3_K already declare vec_dot_type=Q8_K, so mul_mat_id (ggml-cpu.c:1561-1611) already quantizes the input to Q8_K once per matmul. The ONLY redundancy is gate vs up sharing the same cur[6144] input; eliminating one from_float(6144) saves 6144 ops/layer = 0.0027% of the CPU matmul work (from_float total is 0.011% of matmul). down cannot share (its input b_swi[2048,6] is a distinct tensor). The 20ms CPU branch is dominated by Q2_K/Q3_K WEIGHT dequant+vec_dot, not input quant. (2) Prestage 24KB->6KB: inp_cpu D2H is 0.43us transfer; the cited '5ms in_cpu' is sync LATENCY (~263us/layer), not bytes — transfer is 0.16% of it. Shrink saves ~6us/token total. (3) Async join copy precondition is FALSE: part_cpu is allocated by the CPU backend via plain malloc (ggml-backend.cpp:493), NOT cudaHostAlloc, and is never cudaHostRegister'd (only ggml_backend_cuda_register_host_buffer exists but is unused for sched intermediates). cudaMemcpyAsync from pageable memory silently serializes — the exact hazard the hack itself flags. (4) Even if pinned, part_cpu is produced only after the 10.5ms CPU long pole and the join is the terminal op — zero concurrent work to hide the 0.43us copy under. (5) Sync-tax removal is the same class already proven inert: prompt states 'events at n_copies=1 ... helped sync count but not wall-clock.' Upper bound ~38 removable syncs x 3us = 0.1ms -> 27.0->27.1 t/s, and those syncs drain real copy-bound GPU work so they aren't free.
verdicts.what_would_kill_it: Already killed by the code+arithmetic: (a) Q2_K/Q3_K vec_dot_type=Q8_K means input is already Q8-quantized once per matmul, so 'Q8-input-once' removes only a duplicate gate/up quant worth 0.003% of CPU compute; (b) part_cpu is unpinned malloc memory so the async-join's central assumption is invalid and it falls back to a synchronous staging copy; (c) the join copy has nothing to overlap because it is the terminal op after the long-pole CPU branch; (d) the prompt's own prior result (sync-count down, wall-clock flat) directly predicts this merge. The decode critical path is ~20ms CPU weight-dequant matmul + ~19ms copy-bound GPU branch; this hack touches neither.
verdicts.first_experiment: Cheapest refutation, no code change: run the existing build with GGML_SCHED_SYNC_COUNT>=3 and read the [SYNC_TIME] in_cpu bucket already emitted at ggml-backend.cpp:1777. If in_cpu is dominated by latency (per-layer us >> the 0.43us transfer, as predicted), the prestage-shrink and async-join components are confirmed inert before writing anything. To bound the Q8-input-once piece: add a one-line stderr timer (ggml_time_us) around the from_float loop at ggml-cpu.c:1576-1611 in mul_mat_id and sum it per token — expect <0.05ms of the 37ms, i.e. nothing to win. Both are read-only / single-line probes runnable in one decode session.

verdicts.name: Coalesce gate+up+down into one contiguous H2D + drop dead indexer weight from VRAM
verdicts.blocking_issues: The est 30 t/s (+3 over 27) is ~5-6x over-stated; real combined gain is ~0.3-0.5 ms/token => ~27.0-27.4 t/s, inside noise. Refutation grounded in code: (1) copy_experts (ggml-backend.cpp:1647) is PER-TENSOR and already coalesces consecutive expert IDs. The GPU branch copies only n_gpu=8-k=2 experts/layer. The 0.52 GB/token of bytes that cross PCIe (~9 ms at 57 GB/s) are UNCHANGED by coalescing — only the launch overhead is at stake: 114 launches x 3-8us = 0.34-0.91 ms total. Coalescing 114->38 saves at most ~0.2-0.6 ms of launch time, NOT bytes. (2) 'better PCIe fill toward 57 GB/s' is false for these sizes: each existing copy is already 4.1-5.4 MB, far past the ~1 MB knee where Gen5 saturates; they are bandwidth-bound, not launch-bound. (3) The whole GPU branch runs CONCURRENT with the CPU branch, which is the longer pole at k=6 (CPU ~20 ms compute-bound vs GPU-wait ~18-19 ms per the design comment at llama-graph.cpp:1568). Trimming the non-critical branch = ~0 wall-clock. (4) The 'correct-by-construction / placement-layout-only / M effort' claim is wrong: ffn_gate/up/down_exps are 3 separate ggml tensors with distinct base pointers feeding 3 distinct MUL_MAT_ID input tensors. One fused H2D requires re-packing weights into an interleaved [g|u|d]-per-expert layout AND fusing the 3 matmuls — a real graph change. Also gate/up are Q2_K but down is Q3_K (different block sizes 84 vs 110 bytes), so the contiguous region is heterogeneous. (5) Indexer-drop is the only correct half: confirmed ZERO indexer references in llama-graph.cpp and llama-model.cpp; glm-dsa reuses deepseek2::graph (models.h) and never reads them; they sit in VRAM (buft override only matches *exps). But freeing ~0.83 GB buys ~0.28 of a MoE layer host->VRAM => ~1.5% host-read reduction => ~0.3 ms/token. That is the entire real, safe win.
verdicts.what_would_kill_it: It is already dead as a speedup. What kills the headline number: the bytes-per-token across PCIe are invariant under coalescing, and the branch being optimized is off the critical path (CPU branch is the k=6 pole). Even the optimistic 0.6 ms launch saving lands on a non-critical branch => 0 wall gain. If the GPU branch ever became the pole (lower k), the copy is bandwidth-bound on 0.52 GB so launch-count is still in the noise. The coalesce half is additionally killed by the false 'byte-identical/M-effort' premise (needs weight re-pack + 3-matmul fusion + heterogeneous Q2_K/Q3_K handling).
verdicts.first_experiment: Salvage ONLY the indexer-drop half (it is correct and free): add TENSOR_SKIP|TENSOR_NOT_REQUIRED to the 5 indexer create_tensor calls at glm-dsa.cpp:107-111 (already confirmed unreferenced), rebuild, run llama-bench decode batch=1 and compare tok/s + check perplexity is byte-identical (it must be, the tensors are never read). Expected: ~27.0 -> ~27.3 t/s and ~0.83 GB more VRAM headroom — verify with nvidia-smi. Do NOT build the coalesce half; first cheaply instrument whether the GPU branch is even the critical path at k=6 (GGML_SCHED_SYNC_COUNT bucket already exists) — if GPU-wait < CPU branch, coalescing is provably 0 and not worth the re-pack engineering.

verdicts.name: -ub 64 prefill-buffer shrink + correct the phantom non-MLA KV budget to free a resident layer
verdicts.blocking_issues:
The hack's payoff requires freeing ONE resident MoE layer = 2.95 GB on a SINGLE GPU. Both levers together free nowhere near that, so the offloaded-layer count never drops and the per-token CPU expert work is unchanged.

(1) -ub 64 reclaim is real but tiny. Verified from src/llama-context.cpp:410,760: the compute buffer is reserved at worst-case n_tokens=min(n_ctx,n_ubatch). server.sh / server_sweep.sh never set -b/-ub, so a 512-token prefill graph is permanently reserved (dead at decode bs=1). Real buffer sizes from glm51-server.log (same MLA/deepseek2 graph): CUDA0=1168, CUDA1=338, CUDA_Host=40 MiB at ub=512. But only the per-ubatch activations scale with ub (MoE intermediate [exp_dim,8,n_tokens]~32MiB, hidden~12MiB at ub=512; the rest is ub-independent FA/indexer/reuse floor + double-buffering). Estimated ub-scaling portion ~150-500 MiB total; -ub 64 reclaims ~7/8 of that = ~130-450 MiB across BOTH GPUs. Per-GPU that is ~250 MiB (CUDA0) + ~75 MiB (CUDA1).

(2) Phantom-KV correction frees ZERO real VRAM. Verified TRUE that MLA KV is tiny: glm51-server.log shows kv_cache size=702 MiB @ ctx=8192 with V(f16)=0.00 MiB (single latent K head, has_v=false) -> ~351 MiB @ ctx=4096, vs the spec's '8GB KV余裕'. So the spec number is ~20x oversized AS CLAIMED. BUT the 8GB was a planning-doc fudge, not a live allocation: KV is actually allocated split CPU(126)+CUDA0(297)+CUDA1(279) MiB, already ~280-300 MiB/GPU. There is no 8GB block sitting on any GPU to reclaim. The handoff confirms the -ot packing was tuned to MEASURED 94/92 GB usage, not the phantom budget. Correcting a paper number moves no bytes.

(3) Granularity kill: -ot is per whole tensor (ggml_mul_mat_id takes one weight tensor w per backend; src/llama-graph.cpp:1040). The smallest single expert tensor of one offloaded layer is gate_exps Q2_K ~0.98 GB (down Q3_K ~1.29 GB). The ~250 MiB freed on CUDA0 cannot host even ONE of the three expert tensors of one layer, let alone a whole layer (2.95 GB). So offloaded-layer count stays at 19, CPU expert volume per token is unchanged, and the +0.5-0.9 ms/layer saving the hack banks on never occurs.

Against the breakdown: this hack only ever targeted host-read-volume via the layer-count edge. That edge is not crossed. It does nothing for the ~20 ms CPU dequant compute, nothing for the fixed GPU attention/resident work, nothing for cross-backend sync. Net wall-clock change at decode: ~0.
verdicts.what_would_kill_it: Already killed by the arithmetic: combined real VRAM reclaim (~250 MiB on the best GPU) is 6-12x smaller than the 2.95 GB needed for one resident layer, and 4x smaller than the 0.98 GB smallest single expert tensor — so no layer/tensor migrates and the offloaded count is unchanged. It would only be revived if (a) the existing -ot packing were NOT empirically maxed and there were already ~2.7 GB of stranded free VRAM on one GPU that -ub 64 tips over the 2.95 GB threshold — but the handoff's measured 94/92 GB usage says headroom is ~2-4 GB and is the reason CUDA0 already needs ~11 GB staging room, so it is spoken for. There is no plausible world where ~250 MiB closes a 2.95 GB gap.
verdicts.first_experiment: On the already-running persistent server (server_sweep.sh keeps GLM-5.2 loaded), don't even reload: first just read the EXISTING reserve log for the actual GLM-5.2 compute-buffer sizes (grep 'compute buffer size' on the current server.log) to confirm the ub=512 CUDA0/CUDA1 values, then do ONE cheap reload with -ub 64 -b 256 and the SAME -ot string and grep the new 'compute buffer size' lines + free VRAM (nvidia-smi --query-gpu=memory.free). Decision rule: if the freed VRAM on the most-loaded single GPU is < 2950 MiB (it will be ~250-500 MiB), the hack cannot free a layer — stop, do not attempt the -ot remap. Total cost: one ~5-min model load, no code. Expect it to confirm dead.
